problem_summary: 2024 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / climate-and-insurance). The paper answers three linked tasks plus a letter: (1) build a model telling an insurer whether to underwrite property policies in regions with rising extreme weather; (2) adapt that model to advise property developers where/how to build resilient communities; (3) build a model for community leaders to identify at-risk historic landmarks of high cultural/historical/economic value and propose preservation strategies; (4) write a one-page letter to the community. The team built two core models — an actuarial "Climate Risk Assess Model" (Model I) and a "Landmark Preservation Model" (Model II) — bridged by a SEIC real-estate evaluation metric, and demonstrated them on real cities (Mumbai, Cairns; Miami, New Orleans, Houston) and Faneuil Hall in Boston.
models_used: Peaks Over Threshold (POT) extreme-value model with Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD) to fit disaster intensity (loss) tails
Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) for GPD parameters (k=2.929, xi=-0.499); Hill plot for threshold selection (u=2.26)
Hypothesis testing (Kolmogorov-Smirnov) to fit disaster frequency; chose Poisson (lambda_Mumbai=7.458, lambda_Cairns=4.438) over Normal/Uniform/Exponential
Non-homogeneous Poisson via a Logistic growth model dN/dt = aN(1-N/Nmax) to make frequency lambda(t) time-varying and forecast 7 years out
Cramer-Lundberg-style surplus / ruin process with a Brownian-motion disturbance term: U(t)=s+ct-S(t)+beta*W_t, compound Poisson claims S(t)
Ruin theory: Probability of Ruin (POR) computed via the asymptotic tail formula for fat-tailed (GPD) claims, Psi(s)=omega^-1 * Fe(s)
Stochastic optimal control: Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation to derive optimal investment strategy p(s) and optimal excess-of-loss reinsurance retention b(s)
Monte Carlo simulation for data augmentation (expanding sample to 1023 points)
Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for subjective indicator weights in the SEIC metric
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) combined with AHP (AHP-EWM) via Lagrange multiplier / minimum relative information entropy for landmark VOL and ROL weights
Min-max normalization of benefit vs cost attributes; custom Development Potential Index (DPI) = 100*(w_SE*SE + w_I*I + w_C*C)
GE / McKinsey nine-box matrix (VOL vs ROL axes) to map landmarks into preservation-urgency levels A-E
K-Means clustering to partition landmarks within the GE matrix into categories
Q-Q plot against exponential distribution as a fat-tail diagnostic
key_techniques: Tail-aware loss modeling: explicitly diagnosing fat tails (exponential Q-Q plot) and switching from ordinary distribution fitting to POT+GPD instead of naively fitting Lognormal/Gamma to the whole loss distribution

Two-part actuarial decomposition: separately modeling disaster INTENSITY (GPD severity) and FREQUENCY (Poisson count), then composing them into a compound-Poisson surplus process — the standard collective-risk decomposition

Making the Poisson rate time-varying with a logistic curve to encode the problem's core premise (extreme weather is INCREASING) rather than assuming stationarity — directly ties the math to the climate-change narrative
Turning an abstract risk number (POR) into actionable thresholds: four risk levels (I Acceptable <2%, II Tolerable 2-5%, III Cautious 5-12%, IV Unacceptable >12%) with attached decision rules

Model chaining / reuse: POR from Model I is fed in as an input indicator (insurance dimension) of the SEIC metric AND as the 'risk of being uninsured' factor in the landmark model — one engine drives all three tasks
Hybrid subjective+objective weighting (AHP-EWM) to defend against the 'AHP is arbitrary' critique by anchoring weights with data-driven entropy
Borrowing a business-strategy tool (GE/McKinsey matrix) into a conservation context as a creative cross-domain mapping (VOL~market attractiveness, ROL~competitiveness)
Concrete case studies on real, contrasting cities with named data sources (NOAA NCEI, climdex, III.org, FRED, earth.nullschool) to ground every model in real data
Sensitivity analysis done as a 2D parameter sweep (perturb s and alpha_d by 20%, solve 40 POR sets, 3D heatmap) plus noise-injection robustness test on the landmark indicators
why_it_won: "It reads like genuine actuarial science, not a generic optimization template. The judge-pleasing moves: (1) it picked the technically CORRECT tool for catastrophe losses — extreme-value theory (POT/GPD) plus ruin theory plus HJB-optimal reinsurance/investment — which is exactly how real cat-insurance is modeled, signalling deep domain literacy rather than bolting on a neural net; (2) it tightly coupled three sub-models through a single shared quantity (POR), so the paper feels like one coherent system answering all tasks instead of three disconnected mini-papers; (3) it operationalized every result into decision rules and discrete levels (risk levels I-IV, preservation levels A-E) that a real insurer or community leader could act on — strong on the 'so what'; (4) it embedded the problem's climate premise into the math via the logistic time-varying frequency, showing it understood the sustainability theme; (5) it delivered the required letter with a concrete 2024-2030 agenda, cost ranges, and funding sources; and (6) clean structure, named real data sources, and honest sensitivity/robustness checks gave it credibility."
section_highlights: "Summary cleanly previews all three models AND the concrete numeric answers (Mumbai POR 3.71%->12.22% moving level II->III; Cairns 1.33%->4.40% level I->II; DPI ranking Houston>New Orleans>Miami; Faneuil Hall = level A). Keyword line names the actual methods (Ruin Theory; AHP-EWM; GE Matrix; Logistic Model). Section 5 (insurance model) is the technical centerpiece, built in disciplined steps — Step1 data augmentation, Step2 fat-tail exam, Step3 threshold, Step4 MLE — then surplus process -> POR -> HJB optimal strategies -> risk-level table -> case study. The Notation table, Assumptions-with-Justifications, and explicit data-source table all front-load rigor. The GE-matrix figure plotting all landmarks plus Faneuil Hall, and the final one-page letter with a Mermaid-style timeline (2024 assessment -> 2025 design -> 2027 implementation -> 2030 insurance+education) and cost/funding statement, close strongly. Model Evaluation section explicitly lists Strengths (comprehensive disturbance term, GE-matrix creativity, surplus-process rigor) and Weaknesses (ignores inflation/macro; coastal-only; ignores cost of measures)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted: surplus process ignores inflation and macroeconomic shifts; decisions rest only on current economics + weather forecasts
Self-admitted: landmark model only covers COASTAL landmarks (sea-level-rise focus) and measures the EXTENT of preservation needed without costing those measures

Monte Carlo 'data augmentation' to inflate a thin sample to 1023 points is statistically circular — you cannot manufacture genuine tail information from a fitted Lognormal; the GPD is then fit partly to simulated data, weakening the extreme-value claim

GPD shape estimate xi = -0.499 implies a bounded (short, finite-endpoint) tail, which is in tension with the paper's whole 'fat-tailed catastrophe' motivation — a subtle internal inconsistency a sharp judge could flag
AHP judgment matrices are never shown ('for limited space, judgment matrix won't be displayed'), so the subjective weights are unauditable; consistency check is asserted not demonstrated

DPI formula and the VOL/ROL aggregation are linear weighted sums of normalized indicators — convenient but assumes additive, non-interacting factors despite the paper itself drawing a correlation/system-dynamics diagram showing indicators interact
Many reported figures (the extracted chart tables) look idealized/monotonic — predicted exactly equals actual frequency in Figure 9 — suggesting plots may be schematic rather than true fits
Sentence in summary calls Model I both 'not sensitive to market conditions' and the conclusion says model is 'quite sensitive and robust' — contradictory wording around sensitivity
GE-matrix axis labels are described inconsistently in the text ('take VOL as horizontal values and VOL as vertical values'), a proofreading slip in the core deliverable
reusable_patterns: Catastrophe/loss problems: decompose into SEVERITY (extreme-value POT+GPD, MLE, Hill-plot threshold) x FREQUENCY (count distribution via K-S test, usually Poisson) -> compound-Poisson surplus -> probability-of-ruin. This is a transplantable backbone for any insurance/risk MCM problem

Encode a problem's 'increasing trend' premise by making a rate parameter time-varying with a logistic (or other growth) curve — converts a static distribution into a forecasting model and visibly addresses the prompt's dynamics
Build ONE quantitative engine and reuse its output as an input feature across every sub-question (here POR feeds SEIC and the landmark risk) — produces a unified paper and saves modeling effort
Convert continuous risk scores into named discrete tiers with thresholds and attached action rules (risk levels I-IV, preservation levels A-E) to maximize the 'actionable recommendation' credit
Hybrid weighting recipe AHP-EWM (subjective AHP x objective entropy, combined via geometric mean / Lagrange minimum-entropy) as a defensible default for any multi-criteria evaluation index
Repurpose a 2x2 / 3x3 business matrix (GE-McKinsey, BCG) by mapping two computed indices onto its axes to generate a strategy taxonomy — cheap creativity that judges reward
Always pair a custom composite index (like DPI) with explicit benefit-vs-cost min-max normalization and a stated weighting so it is reproducible

Deliverable discipline for ICM: a Summary that states methods AND numeric answers, an Assumptions-with-Justifications block, a data-source table with real URLs, a Strengths/Weaknesses self-critique, and (for Problem E) a real one-page letter with a dated timeline + cost + funding source
Validate with a 2-parameter perturbation sweep visualized as a 3D/heatmap surface plus a separate noise-injection robustness test on the index model
